rows.title: Illinois HB 4711: Artificial Intelligence Systems Regulation Bill Introduced in Illinois General Assembly
rows.summary:
The Illinois General Assembly introduced House Bill 4711, a measure to regulate the development, deployment and use of artificial intelligence systems within the State of Illinois.

On introduction in the Illinois House of Representatives during the current legislative session, HB 4711 was referred to the relevant House committee for consideration. The bill forms part of a broader pattern of state-level legislative activity in the United States addressing AI governance, consumer protection and algorithmic accountability.

HB 4711 proposes requirements applicable to developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating in Illinois. The bill addresses obligations relating to transparency, risk assessment and the protection of Illinois residents from harm arising from automated decision-making. The legislation targets consequential decisions, understood to include determinations affecting employment, housing, credit, education and access to public services, and imposes duties on covered entities to disclose when AI systems are used in such contexts. The bill further provides for impact assessments to be conducted by covered entities prior to deployment of high-risk AI systems, with documentation to be maintained and, in certain circumstances, made available to affected individuals or regulators.

The bill assigns enforcement responsibilities to the Illinois Attorney General, granting that office authority to investigate alleged violations and seek civil penalties against non-compliant entities. HB 4711 also establishes a private right of action allowing Illinois residents to bring claims where they have been harmed by a covered entity's failure to comply with the bill's requirements. Penalty provisions specify financial consequences for violations, though the precise amounts are subject to the final enacted text. The General Assembly determined that existing consumer protection frameworks were insufficient to address the distinct risks posed by AI systems, providing the legislative rationale for a dedicated statutory instrument.

rows.title: Israel National AI Policy and Ethics Framework: Regulatory Guidance on Artificial Intelligence Development and Deployment
rows.summary:
Israel has issued national-level policy guidance on artificial intelligence regulation and ethics, establishing a framework for the development, deployment and oversight of AI systems across the jurisdiction.

In 2023, the Israeli Innovation Authority and the Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology published a national policy document on artificial intelligence regulation and ethics, setting out principles and guidelines applicable to AI developers, operators and public-sector bodies operating in Israel. The document reflects a risk-based approach to AI governance, drawing on international frameworks including those developed by the OECD and the European Union, while addressing the particular characteristics of Israel's technology sector.

The policy document outlines foundational ethical principles governing AI in Israel, including requirements relating to transparency, accountability, fairness, privacy and human oversight. The guidance addresses the responsibilities of entities deploying AI systems to ensure that outputs are explainable and that individuals affected by automated decisions retain meaningful recourse. The document also addresses data governance, setting out expectations regarding the quality and provenance of training data used in AI systems, with reference to existing obligations under Israeli privacy law, including the Protection of Privacy Law 5741-1981 and associated regulations.

The framework identifies categories of higher-risk AI applications, including those deployed in healthcare, financial services, critical infrastructure and law enforcement, and provides sector-specific guidance for each. The Israeli Innovation Authority indicated that the policy is intended to operate alongside, rather than replace, existing sectoral regulation, and that dedicated regulatory instruments may follow for particular high-risk domains. The Ministry stated that the framework is also intended to support Israel's competitiveness in global AI markets by providing developers with regulatory clarity and aligning domestic standards with those of key trading partners.

The guidance does not impose statutory obligations in its current form but represents the authoritative statement of government policy on AI ethics and regulation in Israel. Enforcement of underlying legal obligations, including those arising under privacy, consumer protection and sector-specific legislation, remains with the competent regulatory authorities in each domain.

rows.title: Israel National Program for Artificial Intelligence: Government Resolution on AI Development Strategy
rows.summary:
The Israeli government adopted the National Program for Artificial Intelligence, a multi-year national strategy establishing the framework for AI research, development, and deployment across the Israeli economy and public sector.

On 15 November 2021, the Israeli government approved Resolution No. 569, formally adopting the National Program for Artificial Intelligence under the auspices of the Innovation Authority and the Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology. The programme was developed in coordination with the National Economic Council and is intended to position Israel as a leading AI nation by 2030.

The programme allocates a total budget reported at approximately ILS 1.6 billion (approximately EUR 410 million / USD 450 million at the time of adoption) over multiple years, directed at four principal pillars: advancing AI research and academic infrastructure; developing AI talent and human capital through education initiatives; encouraging commercial AI adoption across industry sectors; and establishing a governance and regulatory environment conducive to responsible AI development. The Resolution provides that a national steering committee, chaired by the Prime Minister's Office, will oversee implementation and coordinate across relevant ministries.

The programme further provides for the establishment of national AI research centres, investment in computing infrastructure to support large-scale AI workloads, and measures to attract and retain AI expertise within Israel. The Resolution identifies specific sectors, including health, agriculture, transportation, and defence, as priority domains for early AI deployment. It also provides for Israel's participation in international AI cooperation frameworks, including engagement with OECD AI policy initiatives and bilateral technology agreements with partner states.

On the regulatory side, the programme directs relevant authorities to examine the need for sector-specific AI guidance and to monitor developments in international AI regulation, including the European Union's legislative process on artificial intelligence, with a view to aligning Israeli standards where appropriate.

rows.title: Israel Privacy Protection Authority Draft Guidelines on AI Systems and Privacy Protection Law
rows.summary:
The Israeli Privacy Protection Authority has published draft guidelines interpreting the applicability of the Privacy Protection Law to artificial intelligence systems, in anticipation of the entry into force of Amendment 13 to that Law in August 2025.

On 11 May 2025, the Authority published the draft guidelines, which set out its interpretation of the Privacy Protection Law in the context of developing and using AI systems and explain how the Authority intends to exercise its supervisory powers in that domain.

On informed consent and notification, the guidelines state that the use of personal data in AI systems requires obtaining informed consent from the data subject. The Authority's position is that valid consent requires presentation of: the purposes for which data is collected; the recipients of the data and the purposes of disclosure; whether provision of data is legally required; the types and sources of data to be used; a description of risks associated with each data-processing purpose; a description of how the AI system will process the data; and disclosure where data is collected via an AI-based automated system that may materially affect consent.

On accountability, the Authority states that accountability practices are of particular importance in AI development and deployment. Under Amendment 13, many organisations are required to appoint a Data Protection Officer, and the Authority's position is that organisations training AI models are 'very likely' to fall within that requirement. The guidelines further provide that conducting a Data Protection Impact Assessment prior to using AI systems to process personal information is the recommended method of demonstrating compliance with privacy protection requirements. The guidelines also address the adoption of internal policies governing the use of generative AI tools, specifying that such policies should cover permitted users, authorisation procedures, permitted tools, categories of data that may be uploaded, prompt retention periods, restrictions on use of input data for algorithm training, and employee training on security risks.

On web scraping, the Authority states that scraping personal data from the internet to train AI models without informed consent from the data subject constitutes unlawful privacy infringement. The guidelines further provide that even where individuals publish data about themselves or others on social media, this does not generally constitute informed consent for AI training purposes. Operators of online platforms are stated to bear an obligation to take steps to prevent scraping of personal data from platforms they manage.

rows.title: Illinois HB 4799: Artificial Intelligence Disclosure Requirements for Employers
rows.summary:
The Illinois General Assembly passed House Bill 4799, amending the Illinois Human Rights Act to impose disclosure and notice obligations on employers using artificial intelligence tools in employment decisions, applicable to employees and job applicants in the State of Illinois.

On 28 May 2024, the Illinois General Assembly passed HB 4799, and Governor JB Pritzker signed the measure into law on 9 August 2024, with the relevant provisions taking effect on 1 January 2026.

The Act amends the Illinois Human Rights Act (775 ILCS 5) to address the use of artificial intelligence in employment-related determinations. Under the amended provisions, employers are prohibited from using artificial intelligence in a manner that results in discrimination against employees or job applicants on the basis of a protected class as defined under the Act. The legislation defines artificial intelligence broadly to encompass machine-based systems that, for a given set of objectives, make predictions, recommendations or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. Covered employment decisions include recruitment, hiring, promotion, discharge, discipline, and terms and conditions of employment.

The Act provides that employers who use artificial intelligence to make, or to a substantial degree influence, covered employment decisions must notify affected employees and applicants of such use. Specifically, employers are required to disclose when AI is used to determine terms and conditions of employment, to evaluate performance, or to screen candidates. The Illinois Department of Human Rights is charged with enforcement and is directed to adopt rules implementing the disclosure framework. Violations of the notice requirements are actionable under the existing complaint and civil enforcement procedures established within the Illinois Human Rights Act, including potential remedies available through the Illinois Human Rights Commission.

The legislation also directs the Department of Human Rights to study the use of artificial intelligence by employers in Illinois and to report findings and recommendations to the General Assembly, with particular attention to whether additional protections against algorithmic discrimination may be warranted.
